text: Hello, my name is Dr. Eric Smith. I'm going to share with you my technique for robotic room wide gastric bypass. I use a technique called camera hopping where I move the camera left and right and you'll see that indicated on the slides upcoming.
This patient had a hiatal hernia that was repaired at the beginning of the procedure and that will be included in another video.

The camera is positioned to the left, providing a view of the surgeon using a vessel sealer to dissect into the retrogastric space. Four trocars are aligned in a straight line above the umbilicus, facilitating the perigastric technique.

The surgeon moves the camera to the right and introduces the SureForm stapler with a blue load to begin creating the gastric pouch. The stapler is angled slightly upwards towards the spleen to form the initial spire of the pouch.

The surgeon dissects approximately five centimeters below the gastroesophageal junction, just beneath the first gastric vessel on the lesser curvature of the stomach, using the tip-up instrument to access the space towards the angle of His.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the phrenogastric vessel attachments using a vessel sealer, finalizing the creation of the gastric pouch. The angle of His has already been dissected due to a previous hiatal hernia repair.

The surgeon inserts a 36 French oral gastric tube into the gastric pouch to calibrate its size, ensuring it is narrow. The final stapling is performed just lateral to the gastroesophageal junction, creating a small, long cylindrical pouch resembling a portion of an upper sleeve.

The surgeon moves the camera to the left side and uses a vessel sealer to dissect small vessels on the posterior aspect of the gastric pouch, preparing it for anastomosis. The dissection is performed posterior to the staple line using cautery or the vessel sealer.

The surgeon elevates the omentum, positioning the patient in a slight reverse Trendelenburg with approximately fifteen degrees flexion of the upper torso. This positioning flattens the upper abdomen, facilitating visualization of the upper foregut structures.

The surgeon gains access to the omentum anterior to the transverse colon and divides it. The ligament of Treitz is identified and run approximately fifty centimeters in a clockwise fashion. The distal end is sutured to the lateral aspect of the gastric pouch using a six-inch, two-oh absorbable V-lock suture.

The surgeon begins suturing laterally on the gastric pouch, running the suture posteriorly to secure the omega loop to the pouch. This forms the first layer of a double-layer hand-sewn anastomosis. The needle is oriented to the left, sometimes placed in a small amount of fat to stabilize it.

The surgeon removes the oral gastric tube from the gastric pouch, using scissors and cautery to create a gastrotomy approximately 25 to 30 millimeters in size. Any visible bleeding is controlled, and a corresponding enterotomy is made on the jejunum directly below the gastrotomy.

The surgeon uses the fourth arm to apply traction and tension, preventing thermal injuries to the posterior aspect. A six-inch absorbable V-lock suture is used to complete the full thickness posterior row, starting at the corner of the opening and running the suture line across the back row.

The surgeon brings the stitch out from inside to outside at the corner of the opening, preparing to tie it to the anterior full thickness layer. The anterior full thickness layer is visible, and the surgeon places one to two throws with the suture before positioning the oral gastric tube for better visibility.

The surgeon guides the tube down into the root limb, which helps splay the tissue and prevent stricture or backwalling of the anastomosis. A six-inch absorbable V-lock suture is used to run across the full thickness of the tissue.
The surgeon ties the suture to the full thickness posterior row, securing it with two or three throws to prevent any loosening or backing up of the suture.

The surgeon uses a six-inch absorbable V-lock suture to imbricate the anterior suture line, completing a two-layered, hand-sewn gastrojejunostomy. The staple line is incorporated or rolled in, depending on the available tissue, and the suture is tied to the initial seromuscular stitch placed posteriorly.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to create a small opening in the mesentery, then fires a white load to complete the anastomosis of the gastrojejunostomy. Following completion, the surgeon prepares to perform a leak test by occluding the anastomosis with the tip up.

The surgeon retracts the oral gastric tube to a position distal to the anastomosis, leaving openings above and below it. A solution of 5 cc's of ICG in 100 cc's of saline is prepared to check for extravasation. The surgeon measures 150 centimeters from the gastrojejunostomy along the jejunum to determine the site for the upcoming jejunojejunostomy.

The surgeon places a 2-0 Vicryl suture to approximate the two intestinal limbs. Scissors with cautery are used to create two small enterotomies in preparation for the jejunojejunostomy. The camera is repositioned to the right, allowing the stapler to enter and perform the jejunojejunostomy using a white load.

The surgeon inspects the staple line for any signs of bleeding. A nine-inch, two-oh absorbable V-lock suture is used to close the jejunal opening. The suture is run in a full-thickness manner down the opening and then back in a seromuscular fashion to achieve a two-layer closure.

The surgeon uses a nine-inch absorbable V-lock suture to perform a two-layer closure of the jejunal opening, ensuring no stricturing occurs due to the hand-sewn technique. An interrupted vicryl suture is placed at the tail of the suture to relieve tension from the barbs, followed by a Brolin stitch similar to those used in laparoscopic surgeries.

The surgeon closes the mesenteric defect using a two O Ethabond suture, employing a purse-string technique with a small slip knot for secure closure. Arista powder is applied along the staple line to assist in achieving hemostasis.
The surgeon completes the Roux-en-Y gastric bypass procedure, finalizing the surgical steps and ensuring all anastomoses are secure.
